output_text:
{"covered_dims": {"R1.1": 0.8, "R1.2": 0.9, "R1.3": 0.9, "R1.4": 0.7, "R1.5": 0.95, "R1.6": 0.7, "R1.7": 0.4}}

Summary: Reorganized the section into a conventional intro flow (problem → gap → contribution → results → model sketch), tightened prose, and flagged several substantive issues — most importantly that the abstract/intro currently leads with the method before establishing the problem, and that p_LLM is defined in a way that may be circular.

## Substantive issues to address

**1. Ordering is inverted.** You open with "We present CrowdMix…" and only reach the motivating contamination problem in paragraph 3. A reader who doesn't already know LLM contamination is a problem cannot evaluate why this method matters. The natural order is: (a) contamination phenomenon and stakes, (b) limitation of existing annotator models, (c) what CrowdMix adds, (d) headline result, (e) formal sketch.

**2. p_LLM is under-specified and potentially circular.** You say p_LLM is "estimated from a held-out auto-generated reference set." Two problems:
   - The reader doesn't know which LLM, how many samples per item, or how the reference set relates to the worker pool's likely models. If workers use GPT-4o and your reference set is generated by Llama-3, the mixture identifies the wrong thing.
   - If p_LLM is a point estimate plugged into the likelihood, you're conditioning on an empirical distribution as if it were known — understating posterior uncertainty on θ_i. State this assumption explicitly or model p_LLM with its own posterior.

**3. The "4.7 / 3.1 F1 points" claim needs scaffolding.** You report gains on two tasks but don't say (a) what the baseline F1 was, (b) how many gold items, (c) whether the gain is statistically meaningful, or (d) what happened on the other four tasks. Selectively reporting two of six tasks invites the reader to assume the other four were null or negative.

**4. Identifiability is unaddressed.** A mixture of "LLM distribution" and "worker confusion matrix conditioned on gold" can trade off against each other — especially when the worker's confusion matrix is diagonal-heavy and p_LLM also concentrates on the correct answer. A sentence on what breaks the symmetry (the held-out reference set? gold anchors? prior tightness?) would preempt an obvious reviewer objection.

**5. Citations are missing throughout.** Dawid & Skene is named but uncited; the "30–46%" measurement is uncited; item-response and hierarchical-pooling extensions are alluded to without pointers; OPEN-CROWD-2024 needs a citation. As written this reads like a draft pre-bibliography pass.

**6. Novelty framing is thin.** "None of these models explicitly accommodates a contaminating generative process" is the entire delta claim. Consider naming one or two recent works that *do* attempt LLM-detection on crowd data (there are several from 2023–2024) and saying precisely how CrowdMix differs — joint inference vs. post-hoc filtering, per-worker vs. per-item, etc.

**7. Minor prose.** "downstream consumers of crowdsourced labels" is wordy → "downstream users." "a contamination loop that motivates explicit modeling" is a fragment-ish appendage; fold it into the prior clause. Hyperprior `Gamma(2, 0.5)` should specify shape/rate vs. shape/scale convention.

## Suggested revised draft

> The widespread availability of instruction-tuned LLMs has changed the crowdsourcing landscape: recent measurements estimate that 30–46% of MTurk text-production submissions contain LLM output [CITE], threatening the validity of datasets used to train and evaluate the very models doing the contaminating.
>
> Bayesian models of annotator behavior, from Dawid and Skene's confusion-matrix model [CITE] through item-response [CITE] and hierarchical-pooling [CITE] extensions, treat every response as a draw from the annotator's own judgment process. None accommodates a second, contaminating generative process. Recent work on detecting LLM-generated crowd responses [CITE] operates post-hoc at the submission level and does not produce per-worker contamination estimates usable for reweighting.
>
> We present **CrowdMix**, a hierarchical mixture model that jointly infers per-annotator LLM-use rates and underlying label distributions from crowdsourced annotations. Each annotator *i* has a latent LLM-use propensity θ_i ~ Beta(α, β), with hyperpriors α, β ~ Gamma(2, 0.5) (shape, rate). Each annotation a_{ij} is drawn from a mixture: with probability θ_i from a shared LLM response distribution p_LLM(·|j) estimated from a reference set generated by [MODEL, N samples/item], and with probability 1−θ_i from annotator *i*'s confusion matrix conditioned on latent gold label z_j ~ Categorical(π). Identifiability rests on [the reference set anchoring p_LLM / a small gold-anchored subset / prior concentration on diagonal confusion]. We fit the model in NumPyro via NUTS.
>
> On the AmazonMTurk-Annotated subset of OPEN-CROWD-2024 [CITE] (12,440 annotations across 6 text-production tasks), CrowdMix estimates a posterior mean LLM-use rate of 0.34 (95% HDI [0.29, 0.39]) with substantial cross-worker heterogeneity (σ_w = 0.21). On the held-out gold subset, reweighting majority vote by posterior (1−θ_i) improves F1 over plain majority vote on [X of 6] tasks, with the largest gains on paraphrase (+4.7) and summarization (+3.1); full results in §[N].

## Uncertainty disclosure
- I do not know the actual citation keys for Dawid & Skene, OPEN-CROWD-2024, the 30–46% measurement, or the recent LLM-detection-on-crowd work — bracketed `[CITE]` placeholders need to be filled by you.
- The identifiability concern (point 4) is my inference from the model spec, not a verified failure mode; a quick simulation with a diagonal worker matrix and a peaked p_LLM would confirm or rule it out.
- Convention for `Gamma(2, 0.5)` (rate vs. scale) is genuinely ambiguous in your text; I assumed rate in the rewrite but you should set it to whatever NumPyro is actually parameterized with.
